subject: Physics
q: The figure shows an experimental plot for discharging of a capacitor in an $R-C$ circuit. The time constant $\tau$ of this circuit lies between:
question: The figure shows an experimental plot for discharging of a capacitor in an $R-C$ circuit. The time constant $\tau$ of this circuit lies between:
solution:
For discharging of an RC circuit,
$V=V_0 e^{-t / \tau}$
So, when
$V=\frac{V_0}{2}$
$\frac{\mathrm{V}_0}{2}=\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{t} / \tau}$
$\ln \frac{1}{2}=-\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\tau} \Rightarrow \tau=\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\ln 2}$
From graph when $\mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_0}{2}, \mathrm{t}=100 \mathrm{~s} \quad \therefore \tau=\frac{100}{\ln 2}=144.3 \mathrm{~sec}$

subject: Physics
q: A capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> has energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is connected to another capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>C</mi></math> and potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>V</mi></math>. Then the loss of energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mi>E</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is _______.
question: A capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> has energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>E</mi><mo>.</mo></math> It is connected to another capacitor of capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>C</mi></math> and potential <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>V</mi></math>. Then the loss of energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac><mi>E</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is _______.
solution:
Given, E=12CV2

Required energy loss 

=12C1C2C1+C2V1-V22=12C×2CC+2CV-2V2

=26CV2=23·E

∴x=2

given ϵ0=8.86×10-12C2/Nm2
solution:
Electric field inside capacitor =Vd
=VdAkε0Akε0=VCAkε0∵kAε0d=C
106=500×15×10-1210-4×k×8.85×10-12
k=15×5008.85×10-4×106=758.85
k=8.47≈8.5

question:
A capacitor is connected to a 20 V battery through a resistance of 10Ω. It is found that the potential difference across the capacitor rises to 2 V in 1 μs. The capacitance of the capacitor is ________μF.
Given In 109=0.105
solution:
V=V01-e-t/RC

2=201-e-1μs10C

110=1-e-1×10-610C

e-1×10-610C=910

1×10-610C=ln109

C=1×10-610×ln109=10-71.05=11.05μF=100105μF=0.95μF

q: A parallel plate capacitor with square plates is filled with four dielectrics of dielectric constants K1, K2, K3, K4 arranged as shown in the figure. The effective dielectric constant K will be:
question: A parallel plate capacitor with square plates is filled with four dielectrics of dielectric constants K1, K2, K3, K4 arranged as shown in the figure. The effective dielectric constant K will be:
solution:
C1 and C2 are in series.

The equivalent capacitance of C1 and C2 is

C12=∈0A2d2K1+d2K2=∈0AdK1K2K1+K2

C3 and C4 are in series

C34=∈0A2d2K3+d2K4=∈0AdK3K4K3+K4

Ceq=C12+C34=∈0AdK1K2K1+K2+K3K4K3+K4

∴Keq=K1K2K1+K2+K3.K4K3+K4

q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor is charged fully by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply. It is then disconnected from the supply and is connected in series to another uncharged <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor. If the energy change during the charge redistribution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mn>100</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> to the nearest integer is :
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor is charged fully by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>220</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> supply. It is then disconnected from the supply and is connected in series to another uncharged <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μF</mi></math> capacitor. If the energy change during the charge redistribution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mn>100</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math> then value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> to the nearest integer is :
solution:
C1=5μF  V1=220Volt

C2=2.5μF  V2=0

Heat loss;

ΔH=U1-Ut=12C1C2C1+C2v1-v22

=12×5×2.5(5+2.5)(220-0)2μJ

=52×3×22×22×100×10-6J

=5×11×223×10-4J=55×223×10-4J

=12103×10-4J=12103×10-3J=4×10-2

According to questions

x100=4×10-2

so, x=4

subject: Physics
q: The equivalent capacitance between $A$ and $B$ in the circuit given below is:
question: The equivalent capacitance between $A$ and $B$ in the circuit given below is:
solution:
The simplified circuit of the circuit given in question as follows:
The equivalent capacitance between C \& D capacitors of $2 \mu \mathrm{F}, 5 \mu \mathrm{F}$ and $5 \mu \mathrm{F}$ are in parallel. $\therefore \quad \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{CD}}=2+5+5=12 \mu \mathrm{F}(\because$ In parallel grouping $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2+\ldots .+\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}}$ ) Similarly equivalent capacitance between $\mathrm{E}$ $\& \mathrm{BC}_{\mathrm{EB}}$ $=4+2=6 \mu \mathrm{F}$ Now equivalent capacitance between A \& B $\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{1}{6}+\frac{1}{12}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{5}{12}$ $\Rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{12}{5}=2.4 \mu \mathrm{F}(\because$ In series grouping, $\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_2}+\ldots \ldots \ldots+\frac{1}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{n}}}$ )

q:
Two identical parallel plate capacitors, of capacitance C each, have plates of area A, separated by a distance d . The space between the plates of the two capacitors, is filled with three dielectrics, of equal thickness and dielectric constants K1, K2 and K3 . The first capaciitor is filled as shown in figure I, and the second one is filled as shown in figure II.

question:
Two identical parallel plate capacitors, of capacitance C each, have plates of area A, separated by a distance d . The space between the plates of the two capacitors, is filled with three dielectrics, of equal thickness and dielectric constants K1, K2 and K3 . The first capaciitor is filled as shown in figure I, and the second one is filled as shown in figure II.
If these two modified capacitors are charged by the same potential V , the ratio of the energy stored in the two, would be (E1 refers to capacitor I and E2 to capacitor (II) ):
solution:
Energy stored in a capacitor E=12CV2
⇒E1E2=C1C2

C1=d3 ε0k1A+d3ε0Ak2+d3ε0Ak3-1
=d3Aε0-11k1+1k2+1k3-1
=d3Aε0-1k1k2+k2k3+k3k1k1k2k3-1

C1=3Aε0dk1k2k3k1k2+k2k3+k3k1
C2=ε0A3k1d+ε0A3k2d+ε0A3k3d
∴C1C2=E1E2=9 k1k2k3k1+k2+k3k1k2+k2k3+k3k1

q: Two capacitors of capacitances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>C</mi></math> are charged to potential differences <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>V</mi></math>, respectively. These are then connected in parallel in such a manner that the positive terminal of one is connected to the negative terminal of the other. The final energy of this configuration is :
question: Two capacitors of capacitances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>C</mi></math> are charged to potential differences <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>V</mi></math>, respectively. These are then connected in parallel in such a manner that the positive terminal of one is connected to the negative terminal of the other. The final energy of this configuration is :
solution:
Charge on C=CV
Charge on 2C=2C2V

From charge conservation, 2C(2V)-CV=(C+2C)V'
Common potential, V'=V
Final energy, Uf=12CV2+12×2CV2=32CV2

E = 3 × 1 0 4  V/m

We know that

Q = CV

   =kε0Ad·E·d

    =kε0AE

  QA=kε0E

=2.2×8.85×10-12×3×104

=58.41×10-8

=5.84×10-7

≈6×10-7C/m2

subject: Physics
q:
Identify the correct statements :
A. Effective capacitance of a series combination of capacitors is always smaller than the smallest capacitance of the capacitor in the combination.
B. When a dielectric medium is placed between the charged plates of a capacitor, displacement of charges cannot occur due to insulation property of dielectric.
C. Increasing of area of capacitor plate or decreasing of thickness of dielectric is an alternate method to increase the capacitance.
D. For a point charge, concentric spherical shells centered at the location of the charge are equipotential surfaces.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Identify the correct statements :
A. Effective capacitance of a series combination of capacitors is always smaller than the smallest capacitance of the capacitor in the combination.
B. When a dielectric medium is placed between the charged plates of a capacitor, displacement of charges cannot occur due to insulation property of dielectric.
C. Increasing of area of capacitor plate or decreasing of thickness of dielectric is an alternate method to increase the capacitance.
D. For a point charge, concentric spherical shells centered at the location of the charge are equipotential surfaces.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
A. Effective capacitance of series combination is always smaller than the smallest capacitance - TRUE. For series: $\frac{1}{C_{eff}} = \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} + \ldots$, therefore $C_{eff} < C_{min}$
B. Dielectric prevents charge displacement - FALSE. A dielectric allows polarization (displacement of bound charges) but prevents free charge movement. It does not prevent displacement of bound charges.
C. Increasing area or decreasing thickness increases capacitance - TRUE. From $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$, increasing $A$ or decreasing $d$ increases $C$.
D. Concentric spherical shells centered at a point charge location are equipotential surfaces - TRUE. For a point charge, the potential at distance $r$ is $V = \frac{kq}{r}$, which depends only on distance, making concentric spheres equipotential surfaces.
Correct statements: A, C, D

subject: Physics
q:
From the circuit given below, the capacitance between terminals $A$ and $B$ shown in the circuit is ______ $\mu$F.
(take $C_1=C_2=C_3=1$ $\mu$F and $C_4=2$ $\mu$F.)
question:
From the circuit given below, the capacitance between terminals $A$ and $B$ shown in the circuit is ______ $\mu$F.
(take $C_1=C_2=C_3=1$ $\mu$F and $C_4=2$ $\mu$F.)
solution:
Let the nodes in the circuit be labeled to identify the connections:
- Let the input terminal be Node A.
- Let the junction between $C_1$ and $C_2$ be Node X.
- The top wire connects Node A to the junction between $C_2$ and $C_3$. Thus, this junction is also at the potential of Node A.
- The bottom wire connects Node X to the output terminal Node B, with capacitor $C_4$ in this path.

Based on these nodes, the connections of each capacitor are as follows:
- $C_1$ is connected between Node A and Node X.
- $C_2$ is connected between Node X and Node A.
- $C_3$ is connected between Node A and Node B.
- $C_4$ is connected between Node X and Node B.

We can now simplify the circuit step-by-step:
Capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ are in parallel between nodes A and X. Their equivalent capacitance is:
$C_{12} = C_1 + C_2 = 1\,\mu\text{F} + 1\,\mu\text{F} = 2\,\mu\text{F}$

This equivalent capacitor $C_{12}$ is in series with $C_4$ (which is connected between X and B). The equivalent capacitance of this branch is:
$C_{AXB} = \dfrac{C_{12} \times C_4}{C_{12} + C_4} = \dfrac{2 \times 2}{2 + 2} = 1\,\mu\text{F}$

Finally, this branch is in parallel with $C_3$ (which is connected directly between A and B). The total equivalent capacitance between A and B is:
$C_{AB} = C_{AXB} + C_3 = 1\,\mu\text{F} + 1\,\mu\text{F} = 2\,\mu\text{F}$

Answer: $2$

subject: Physics
q: Two capacitors having capacitance C1 and C2 respectively are connected as shown in figure. Initially, capacitor C1 is charged to a potential difference V volt by a battery. The battery is then removed and the charged capacitor C1 is now connected to uncharged capacitor C2 by closing the switch S. The amount of charge on the capacitor C2, after equilibrium, is
question: Two capacitors having capacitance C1 and C2 respectively are connected as shown in figure. Initially, capacitor C1 is charged to a potential difference V volt by a battery. The battery is then removed and the charged capacitor C1 is now connected to uncharged capacitor C2 by closing the switch S. The amount of charge on the capacitor C2, after equilibrium, is
solution:
The initial charge on the capacitor C1 will be Q=C1V. If after closing the  switch the charge flown through the circuit is q, the charge on C2 will be q and C1 will be Q-q. The potential across the capacitors will be same. Hence,

Q-qC1=qC2⇒q=C2QC1+C2⇒q=C1C2C1+C2V

subject: Physics
q: Consider a circuit consisting of a capacitor ($20\ \mu$F), resistor ($100\ \Omega$) and two identical diodes as shown in figure. The resistance of diode under forward biasing condition is $10\ \Omega$. The time constant of the circuit is $\alpha \times 10^{-3}$ s. The value of $\alpha$ is _____
question: Consider a circuit consisting of a capacitor ($20\ \mu$F), resistor ($100\ \Omega$) and two identical diodes as shown in figure. The resistance of diode under forward biasing condition is $10\ \Omega$. The time constant of the circuit is $\alpha \times 10^{-3}$ s. The value of $\alpha$ is _____
solution:
From the given circuit diagram, the battery is connected such that its positive terminal is upwards. This means the current will flow in the clockwise direction during the charging of the capacitor.

In the parallel combination of the two diodes, the left diode is pointing downwards (in the direction of the current) and will be forward-biased. The right diode is pointing upwards (opposite to the current) and will be reverse-biased.

The reverse-biased diode offers infinite resistance and acts as an open circuit. The forward-biased diode offers a resistance of $10\ \Omega$.

The total equivalent resistance of the circuit is the sum of the resistor $R$ and the resistance of the forward-biased diode:
$R_{eq} = 100\ \Omega + 10\ \Omega = 110\ \Omega$

The time constant $\tau$ of the $RC$ circuit is given by:
$\tau = R_{eq}C$
$\tau = 110 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$
$\tau = 2200 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 2.2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}$

Comparing this with the given time constant $\alpha \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}$, we get:
$\alpha = 2.2$

Answer: $2.2$

subject: Physics
q:
The electric field between the two parallel plates of a capacitor of $1.5 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitance drops to one third of its initial value in $6.6 \mu \mathrm{s}$ when the plates are connected by a thin wire.
The resistance of this wire is _____$\Omega$. (Given, $\log 3=1.1$ )
question:
The electric field between the two parallel plates of a capacitor of $1.5 \mu \mathrm{F}$ capacitance drops to one third of its initial value in $6.6 \mu \mathrm{s}$ when the plates are connected by a thin wire.
The resistance of this wire is _____$\Omega$. (Given, $\log 3=1.1$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_0}{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}=\frac{\mathrm{V}_0}{3} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{V}_0}{3}=\mathrm{V}_0 \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\tau}} \\ & \mathrm{t}=\tau \ell \mathrm{n} 3 \\ & 6.6 \times 10^{-6}=\mathrm{R}\left(1.5 \times 10^{-6}\right)(1.1) \\ & \mathrm{R}=\frac{6}{1.5}=4 \Omega\end{aligned}$

q: In an electrical circuit drawn below the amount of charge stored in the capacitor is _______ μC.
question: In an electrical circuit drawn below the amount of charge stored in the capacitor is _______ μC.
solution:

Therefore, charge on capacitor qc=CVc=10×6=60 μC

subject: Physics
q: The space between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C (without any dielectric) is now filled with three dielectric slabs of dielectric constants $K_{1}=2, K_{2}=3$ and $K_{3}=5$ (as shown in figure). If new capacitance is $\frac{n}{3} C$ then the value of $n$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: The space between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C (without any dielectric) is now filled with three dielectric slabs of dielectric constants $K_{1}=2, K_{2}=3$ and $K_{3}=5$ (as shown in figure). If new capacitance is $\frac{n}{3} C$ then the value of $n$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
The initial capacitance is $C = \frac{\epsilon_0 A}{d}$.
From the figure, the capacitor is divided into three parts.
The bottom half is divided into two capacitors $C_2$ and $C_3$ in parallel, each with area $A/2$ and thickness $d/2$.
$C_2 = \frac{K_2 \epsilon_0 (A/2)}{d/2} = K_2 \frac{\epsilon_0 A}{d} = 3C$
$C_3 = \frac{K_3 \epsilon_0 (A/2)}{d/2} = K_3 \frac{\epsilon_0 A}{d} = 5C$
The equivalent capacitance of the bottom part is $C_{eq, bottom} = C_2 + C_3 = 3C + 5C = 8C$.
The top half is a single capacitor $C_1$ with area $A$ and thickness $d/2$.
$C_1 = \frac{K_1 \epsilon_0 A}{d/2} = 2K_1 \frac{\epsilon_0 A}{d} = 2(2)C = 4C$.
The total capacitance $C'$ is the series combination of $C_1$ and $C_{eq, bottom}$.
$\frac{1}{C'} = \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_{eq, bottom}} = \frac{1}{4C} + \frac{1}{8C} = \frac{2+1}{8C} = \frac{3}{8C}$
$C' = \frac{8}{3}C$
Comparing with $C' = \frac{n}{3}C$, we get $n = 8$.

q: A parallel plate capacitor has plate area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and plate separation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The space between the plates is filled up to a thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with a material of dielectric constant of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo></math> The resultant capacitance of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>pF</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> to the nearest integer is ______.
question: A parallel plate capacitor has plate area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and plate separation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The space between the plates is filled up to a thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with a material of dielectric constant of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>.</mo></math> The resultant capacitance of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi>pF</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>ε</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>85</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>12</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">F</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> to the nearest integer is ______.
solution:
Here, Plate areaA=100 m2
Using, Capacity of parallel plate capacitor is,

C=kε0 A d; where d is separation between the plates and k is dielectric constant.

The capacitance with dielectric will be,
C1=10ϵ0(100)5
=200ϵ0

The capacitance without dielectric or air will be,
C2=ϵ0(100)5=20ϵ0
C1 & C2 are in series so,

 Ceqv. =C1C2C1+C2

=4000ϵ0220
=160.9×10-12≃161pF

q: A capacitor of 50μF is connected in a circuit as shown in figure. The charge on the upper plate of the capacitor is _________ μC.
question: A capacitor of 50μF is connected in a circuit as shown in figure. The charge on the upper plate of the capacitor is _________ μC.
solution:
At steady state current through branch of capacitor is zero. Potential difference across capacitor is 2 V. So, q=50×2=100μC

i=6(2+2+2)×103=1×10-3Amp
VA,B= potential across 50μF
VA,B=i×2×103=1×10-3×2×103=2 volt
Charge on 50μF
q=CV=50×10-6×2⇒100×10-6C=100μC

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor has plate of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>, width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>w</mi></math> and separation of plates is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>. It is connected to a battery of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math>. A dielectric slab of the same thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> and of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> is being inserted between the plates of the capacitor. At what length of the slab inside plates, will the energy stored in the capacitor be two times the initial energy stored?
question: A parallel plate capacitor has plate of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math>, width <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>w</mi></math> and separation of plates is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>. It is connected to a battery of emf <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math>. A dielectric slab of the same thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> and of dielectric constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> is being inserted between the plates of the capacitor. At what length of the slab inside plates, will the energy stored in the capacitor be two times the initial energy stored?
solution:
Given,

Length of plate of parallel plate capacitor is l

Width of plate is w

Separation of plates is d.

Emf of battery is V

Thickness of dielectric slab is d

Dielectric constant of slab is K = 4

Capacitance of capacitor before the insertion of dielectric slab is 

C0=Aε0d=wlε0d  ...(1)

Energy stored in the capacitor before the insertion of dielectrics is given by,

U0=12C0V2=12wlε0dV2  ...(2)

Suppose that, the slab is inside the plate at y distance, therefore, it becomes the combination of two capacitors one with air with length (l-y) and another with dielectric with length y as shown in diagram.

Now the capacitance of capacitor in parallel combination is given by,

C=C1+C2  ...(3)

Where,

C1=w(l-y)ε0d  ...(4)

C1=wyKε0d  ...(5)

Now energy stored in this combination of capacitor is given by

U=12(C1+C2)V2  ...(6)

Now according to the given condition,

2U0=U

⇒212C0V2=12C1+C2V2

⇒2C0=C1+C2

⇒2ϵ0wld=ϵ0Kwyd+ϵ0w(l-y)d

⇒2l=Ky+l-y

⇒l=4y-y=3y

⇒y=l3

Ceq=C+C

subject: Physics
q:
The charge accumulated on the capacitor connected in the following circuit is ______μC.

(Given C=150 μF)
question:
The charge accumulated on the capacitor connected in the following circuit is ______μC.

(Given C=150 μF)
solution:
Under the balanced condition, the capacitor will act as an open circuit.

The current i1 through the 1 Ω resistor is given by

i1=10 V1 Ω+2 Ω=103 A

The current i2 through the 6 Ω resistor is given by

i2=10 V6 Ω+4 Ω=1 A

From the above diagram, it can be written that

VA+i1×1-i2×6=VB⇒VA-VB=1×6-103×1=83 V

Hence, the charge on the capacitor is given by

Q=CVA-VB=150 μF×83 V=400 μC

q: Three capacitances, each of 3 μ F , are provided. These cannot be combined to provide the resultant capacitance of :
question: Three capacitances, each of 3 μ F , are provided. These cannot be combined to provide the resultant capacitance of :
solution:
Equivalent capacitance will be maximum for parallel combination and Minimum for series combination

∴  Possibilities are

1)            C = 1 μ F

2)            C = 9 μ F

3)          1 C = 1 6 + 1 3 = 3 6

                                     ∴    C = 2 μ F

4) ​         C = C 2 + C = 3 2 × C

                                        = 3 2 = 1.5  μ F

subject: Physics
q: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance $1 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ is charged to a potential difference of 20 V . The distance between plates is $1 \mu \mathrm{~m}$. The energy density between plates of capacitor is.
question: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance $1 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ is charged to a potential difference of 20 V . The distance between plates is $1 \mu \mathrm{~m}$. The energy density between plates of capacitor is.
solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { Energy density } & =\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0\left(\frac{V}{d}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(8.85 \times 10^{-12}\right)\left(\frac{20}{10^{-6}}\right)^2 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^3 \\ & \simeq 1.8 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^3\end{aligned}$

solution:
Q=CV
The slope of graph will give the equivalent capacitance of the combination.
From graph A: C1+C2=50010=50 …(i)
From graph B: C1C2C1+C2=8010=8 …(ii)
From equation (i) & (ii)
C1C2=400 …(iii)
C1+400C1=50 ⇒C12-50C1+400=0
C1=50±2500-16002=50±302
=10 μF,  40 μF
